parentQuestion: Generally, what is the result of the negative feedback process?

scaffolds.q: Feedback mechanisms help the body respond to changes.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The body constantly monitors conditions and adjusts its responses.

scaffolds.q: NEGATIVE feedback OPPOSES changes from the normal state.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! 'Negative' means the response is opposite to the change.

scaffolds.q: A thermostat is an example of negative feedback.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Room gets too hot, AC turns on. Gets too cold, heater turns on. Opposes deviations.

scaffolds.q: Blood sugar regulation uses negative feedback.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Too high glucose? Insulin lowers it. Too low? Glucagon raises it.

scaffolds.q: HOMEOSTASIS is the body's stable internal environment.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Temperature, pH, blood pressure - all kept within normal ranges.

scaffolds.q: Negative feedback is the PRIMARY way the body maintains homeostasis.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! By opposing changes, negative feedback keeps conditions stable.

scaffolds.q: Most body processes use negative feedback to maintain stability.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Temperature, blood pressure, pH, glucose - all use negative feedback.

scaffolds.q: The result of negative feedback is HOMEOSTASIS - stable internal conditions.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! By opposing deviations, negative feedback maintains a stable state.
Yes! Negative feedback opposes changes to keep the body stable.
